title: DSDS Guidelines Document Block
description: Actionable usage guidance for an artifact. Each item is a self-contained rule paired with a rationale. Guidelines tell the reader what to do (or not do) and why. They sit alongside other document block kinds in the `documentBlocks` array on any artifact.

guidelineEntry.description: A single piece of usage guidance. Each guideline pairs a statement with a rationale. `level` sets the RFC 2119 enforcement level. `category` groups by discipline. `target` points to a specific anatomy part or content area. `criteria` holds testable success criteria that verify the rule, the test-definition layer of the documentation. `references` cites the external standards (such as WCAG) the rule addresses.

guidance.description: The guidance statement. MUST be concrete and unambiguous. Avoid vague phrases like 'use sparingly' or 'when possible'.

rationale.description: Why this guidance exists. Cite evidence, accessibility standards, or user research when available. MUST NOT restate the guidance.

level.description: How strictly the guideline should be followed, named for RFC 2119 requirement levels: 'must' (non-compliance is a defect), 'should' (follow in most cases; exceptions need justification), 'should-not' (avoid unless justified), 'must-not' (violations are defects). Agents treat must/must-not entries as hard constraints when generating or reviewing code.

evidence.description: Quantitative or empirical backing for this guideline: test run data, audit findings, issue references, or cited sources. Keeping evidence separate from rationale lets tools tell test-derived rules apart from conventional guidance. Example: '9/10 agent test runs failed touch-target minimums when height was overridden below 36px.'

category.description: The discipline this guideline belongs to. Used to group guidelines by concern. Standard values: 'visual-design' (color, typography, spacing, layout), 'interaction' (behavior, animation, transitions, gestures), 'accessibility' (inclusive design, assistive tech, WCAG), 'content' (text, imagery, tone, voice, labeling), 'motion' (animation timing, easing, reduced-motion), 'development' (implementation patterns, performance, code conventions). Custom values are allowed and SHOULD be lowercase kebab-case.

target.description: The anatomy part or content area this guideline applies to (e.g., 'label', 'icon', 'helper-text'). When omitted, the guideline applies to the whole artifact.

criteria.description: Testable success criteria that verify this guideline. Each criterion pairs a stable identifier with an objectively verifiable statement. This lets tooling and agents check implementations against the rule and report pass/fail. Attach criteria only when the success condition is objectively verifiable. Observed results belong in `evidence`.

references.description: External standards this guideline addresses (e.g., WCAG success criteria, MDN or platform guidelines). Each entry cites a URL with an optional display label.

examples.description: Examples showing encouraged and discouraged approaches.

tags.description: Freeform keywords that relate guidelines across categories and disciplines (e.g., 'rtl', 'localization', 'validation', 'contrast').

guidelines.description: Actionable usage rules for an artifact. Each item pairs a guidance statement with a rationale. Categorize by discipline, target a specific anatomy part, and link to external standards like WCAG. Use `guidelines` to answer *how* to use the artifact correctly: concrete do/don't rules, each with a rationale. For *whether* to use it at all, see `useCases`. For the higher-level beliefs behind the rules (on foundations), see `principles`. Guidelines are the home for general documentation guidance in every discipline — including accessibility rules (category 'accessibility') and content rules (category 'content'); the `accessibility` and `content` blocks are reserved for specialized, structured data.

items.description: The guideline rules. Each item is a guidance statement paired with a rationale. Order matters, so tools SHOULD display guidelines in this order. Authors SHOULD order by importance or group by category.

kind.description: Identifies this document block as a guidelines spec.
